metadata.summary: Implement consent management platform with default-deny analytics, Consent Mode v2, GPC signal handling, and IP anonymization. Activate tracking only after explicit consent.

content.title: Privacy-Compliant Analytics
content.summary: Privacy-compliant analytics means implementing a consent management platform (CMP) with default-deny analytics, GA4 Consent Mode v2 wiring, CCPA Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal handling, and IP anonymization — so tracking only activates after explicit user consent. Every new tag or pixel must pass through the consent gate before firing.

why.p: GDPR, UK GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, and LGPD impose fines for analytics cookies set before consent, for "Reject all" buttons that are harder to click than "Accept all" (CNIL fines, 2022–2024), and for US-hosted analytics processing EU personal data without valid SCCs or EU-US DPF. A correct default-deny setup eliminates the most common violations with minimal developer effort.

list.item: Any web or app property tracked by GA4, Plausible, Mixpanel, Amplitude, or PostHog under GDPR, CCPA, or LGPD scope.
Adding a new analytics or marketing tag — must pass through the consent gate before firing.
Migrating to GA4 Consent Mode v2 or upgrading a CMP to IAB TCF 2.2.
Implementing or auditing a CMP: OneTrust, Cookiebot, Iubenda, Osano, CookieYes, Klaro.
DPIA or vendor review for a new tracker, pixel, or session-replay tool.
Internal-only tools behind SSO with no third-party analytics — no public consent surface.
Server-side-only telemetry tied to authenticated user IDs processed under contract (legitimate interest / contract basis) — still needs Records of Processing, but not a cookie banner.
Static brochure sites with zero cookies and zero analytics — a banner here is legal theater.

section.name: consent-gate-setup

rule.statement: Ship the page with analytics_storage: 'denied' and all ad consent signals denied; only update to 'granted' on explicit user action.
rule.rationale: Implied consent ("by continuing to use this site") is not valid consent under GDPR Article 7; default-deny is the only safe starting state in EU/UK jurisdictions.

rule.statement: Queue tracking events fired before consent is granted and flush the queue only after the user explicitly accepts; never drop events silently or fire them before consent.
rule.rationale: Pre-consent events silently dropped cause analytics gaps; pre-consent events fired violate GDPR. Queuing + conditional flush is the correct pattern.

section.reference: AnalyticsManager class: default-deny init, consent grant/revoke, GA4 Consent Mode v2 update, event queue flush, cookie clearing

list.item: analytics_storage: denied by default
ad_storage: denied by default
ad_user_data: denied by default
ad_personalization: denied by default
on explicit accept: update all four to granted (or per-category if granular consent)

rule.statement: Consent Mode v2 still pings Google with cookieless beacons even after "Reject all"; describe this in your privacy policy and verify it aligns with the applicable DPA interpretation.
rule.rationale: CNIL (France) and Austrian DSB have contested cookieless beacons as personal data processing; Consent Mode v2 reduces violation risk but does not eliminate it in all EU jurisdictions.

rule.statement: Check navigator.globalPrivacyControl on page load and honor it as a Do Not Sell / Do Not Share signal without requiring any additional user action.
rule.rationale: CPRA (effective 2023) requires businesses to recognize GPC as a valid opt-out signal; failing to do so is an enforceable violation by the California AG.

list.item: if navigator.globalPrivacyControl === true → gtag('set', 'restricted_data_processing', true)
also honor localStorage 'ccpa_opt_out' for users who explicitly opted out via UI

rule.statement: The "Reject all" button must be one click and visually equal to "Accept all" in size, color, and prominence.
rule.rationale: CNIL (France) issued multiple fines in 2022–2024 for consent banners where "Accept all" was a prominent button and "Reject all" required additional clicks or was visually de-emphasized. Equal prominence is a concrete, auditable requirement.

rule.statement: Every new analytics pixel, SDK, or session-replay tool must have a signed DPA, a valid data-transfer mechanism (SCCs or EU-US DPF), and an entry in the Records of Processing before it fires.
rule.rationale: Adding an undocumented sub-processor violates GDPR Article 28; the CMP consent banner does not authorize processing by unlisted vendors.

list.item: tie the cookie policy table to the live CMP scan output, not a hand-written list — it drifts within weeks
run Cookiebot or Osano quarterly scan to catch new cookies introduced by code changes

rule.statement: Do not claim that IP anonymization exempts analytics from GDPR processing law — state only that it is one mitigation applied, and document the lawful basis separately.
rule.rationale: ICO and EDPB treat anonymized IP-derived analytics as personal data in many cases; claiming anonymization = no GDPR is a common LLM error that creates compliance risk.

antipattern.description: Agent claims "GA4 is GDPR-compliant" as a conclusion.
antipattern.reason: This is a marketing claim, not a legal determination. The correct output is the gating mechanism applied (consent gate + Consent Mode v2 + EU region configuration).

antipattern.description: Agent writes banner copy with highlighted Accept and hidden Reject button.
antipattern.reason: LLMs default to UX patterns optimized for conversion, not consent parity; lock UI parity in the template and do not let the agent restyle.

antipattern.description: Agent claims hashing emails/IDs is sufficient anonymization.
antipattern.reason: Hashing without a rotation policy is reversible; only salted hashing with key rotation can reduce re-identification risk, and even then GDPR may still apply.

antipattern.description: Agent uses Accept-Language or other client-side signal for EU/US/CA region detection.
antipattern.reason: Region detection must use a server-trusted GeoIP signal; client-side headers are user-controlled and not legally reliable for jurisdiction determination.

section.name: pre-launch-checklist

list.item: choose analytics platform(s) and confirm GDPR stance (Plausible/Fathom = cookieless; GA4 = consent required)
install CMP (Cookiebot, Iubenda, CookieYes, or Klaro for OSS)
configure default-deny for analytics_storage and all ad signals
implement Consent Mode v2 defaults before gtag fires
add CCPA GPC signal check (navigator.globalPrivacyControl)
verify "Reject all" is one-click and visually equal to "Accept all"
run automated pre-consent cookie test (see templates/consent-test.ts)

section.reference: Playwright test: no non-essential cookies before consent, GA4 fires only after explicit accept

list.item: confirm GA4 IP anonymization is active (automatic in GA4)
implement server-side IP truncation for custom tracking (remove last IPv4 octet)
never claim hashing == anonymization in documentation or agent output
set GA4 data retention to minimum (2 months for most use cases)
clear user_id on logout: gtag('config', 'G-ID', { user_id: undefined })
implement data access request process (download user data on request)
implement data deletion process and right-to-be-forgotten flag in warehouse
document user rights in privacy policy (access, deletion, rectification, portability)
add users to suppression list on deletion before any campaign sends
maintain versioned consent log: timestamp, policy version, IP hash, user choice
run quarterly CMP cookie scan to catch new cookies from code changes
update cookie policy table from live CMP scan output, not hand-written list
document all sub-processors with DPA and transfer mechanism (SCCs / EU-US DPF)
create incident response plan for data breach (72h GDPR notification requirement)

template.purpose: AnalyticsManager class: consent gate, GA4 Consent Mode v2, CCPA GPC, event queue, cookie clearing
Playwright test: no analytics cookies before consent, GA4 fires only after accept
